post: The skills employers say they need most right now: Critical thinking. Effective communication. Collaboration. Managing disagreement. Ethical reasoning. These skills are civics outcomes. That's the case BRI Director of Strategic Partnerships Jennifer Seal makes, and it's exactly why BRI recently became a proud Investor Member of the PA Chamber of Business and Industry. "Civics provides the knowledge, skills, and habits needed to be responsible and productive  and ones employers are consistently seeking," Seal said. "We believe civics must be prioritized in schools to prepare students to engage in the democratic process, improve their communities, and succeed in their careers." Pennsylvania is a fitting home for this partnership. The United States was established in Philadelphia. The Union was preserved at Gettysburg. Coal, steel, and railroads helped modernize America. The Keystone State has always been at the center of the American story, and BRI is committed to making sure the next generation knows that story and is equipped to carry it forward. 16 days until July 4, 2026. The 250th anniversary is a call to invest in the next 250 years. Read the full piece 👇  https://bit.ly/4uNgA7Q  #PAChamber  #BRI  #Pennysylvania  #A250

250 years ago, the American colonists were trying to figure out what to call themselves. On his own rough cut of the Declaration, Jefferson didnt pen the word citizens.  He wrote "subjects.  And then he crossed out that word and wrote citizens  instead. For well over two centuries, no one knew that Jefferson had first inked the word subjects.  In 2010, the Library of Congress used imaging technology to scrape back the layers of ink. First subjects. Then citizens. That shiftfrom a people defined by power over them to a people who have rights because they are humansis the Declaration's whole argument. And it's where Tony Williams and I started when we sat down with Major Garrett on The Takeout. We talked about what that change set in motion, what it actually cost to move the country closer to its own promise, and why the argument it started is the one that still matters most. Link to the episode in the first comment.

NBC News asked a good question: Do we have the civic education we need for America's 250th? I joined Jon Erwin, the director of Young Washington, to talk about what were doing to make sure the answer is Yes!  The filmhitting theaters July 3rdtells the story of the George Washington you don't know. Not the man of marble but the young man who had to learn from his failures. Young Washington is the center of a new educational initiative the Wonder Project, Stand Together, and the Bill of Rights Institute are bringing to families and classrooms nationwide. The idea is simple: you can't love what you don't know. And a great story is the best way to spark curiosity. Link to the full segment in the first comment.

Grievance 20 is the only grievance in the Declaration aimed at a law passed for another colony. That tells you something about the nature of the fear. The Quebec Act of 1774 replaced English common law with French civil law in Quebec, created a representative system where the king, not the people, appointed the members, and expanded Canadian territory into what is now the Midwest. Individually, each of those provisions alarmed the colonists. Together, they produced something worse: a model of arbitrary government established within immediate proximity to the American colonies. Colonial leaders including Adams, Jay, and Hamilton understood the danger precisely. If Parliament could establish this kind of system in Quebec, nothing prevented it from applying the same logic southward. The expansion of Canadian territory also closed off the westward land colonists believed was essential to their future growth and prosperity, a grievance that connected directly to earlier complaints about the Proclamation Line. Dallas Terry of the Kinder Institute at the University of Missouri explains why the Declaration called the Quebec Act an "example and fit instrument" for introducing absolute rule, and why proximity transformed a law about Canada into a direct threat to colonial liberty. Episode 20 of our 27-part series is live. Join us for Constitution Day Live:  https://bit.ly/4fnlTXp  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #QuebecAct

A right the British called a "second Magna Carta." A clause Lincoln suspended at the start of the Civil War. A tension the United States has never fully resolved. That's the constitutional arc of Grievance 19. Habeas corpus is the right to appear before a judge and hear the charges against you. It had protected English subjects against arbitrary detention since the Magna Carta. The colonists discovered that protection had limits. 1000s of American patriots were transported far from home without due process. Garrett Jones, Postdoc Fellow at the Thomas Jefferson Center at the University of Texas at Austin, explains that Grievance 19 produced the Suspension Clause of the U. S. Constitution. That clause has sat at the center of some of the most consequential debates in American constitutional history. Most famously, Lincoln invoked it unilaterally at the start of the Civil War, after shots were fired on Fort Sumter. How do you balance liberty and security in moments of genuine crisis? Grievance 19 opens that question, and keeps it open. Discover more and join us for Constitution Day Live:  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M New episodes every Monday, Wednesday, and Friday.  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #HabeasCorpus

Trial by jury didn't originate with the American colonists. It originated in 1215. The Magna Carta established that English subjects could not be condemned without judgment by their peers. For more than 5 centuries, that principle had been a cornerstone of English legal tradition, a right colonists had inherited. Which is what made Britain's admiralty court system so pointed a provocation. When Britain needed to enforce taxes and import duties in the colonies after the Seven Years' War, it routed cases through courts where a single magistrate decided guilt. No jury. No deliberation. No verdict that a hostile colonial jury might deliver. Chris Canty of George Washington University explains Grievance 18 through the Liberty Affair of 1768: John Hancock's ship brought before an admiralty court, his lawyer John Adams identifying jury trial deprivation as the core violation even after the case was dismissed. The right the colonists defended mattered enough that the Founders enshrined it specifically in the Sixth and Seventh Amendments, ensuring the admiralty court model would have no place in the republic they were building. Episode 18 of our 27-part series is live. Join us for Constitution Day Live:  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #MagnaCarta  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #USHistory

He started his career in an ambulance. As a paramedic in Nash County, NC, Charles Oliver responded to people in crisis, and kept noticing the same thing: their circumstances were often beyond their control. He started asking a different question. What career could help people before the emergency? The answer was teaching. Today, Oliver teaches "Civic Literacy" at Roanoke Valley Early College, a mixed-grade class of 9th through 12th graders in rural North Carolina. His classroom rule is simple: "We're going to attack opinions. We're not going to attack people." Last month, one of his student groups wrote a song about immigration policy, wrestling with the tension between federal law and human dignity, that ended with this verse: "Searching for justice, trying to find / a balance of law and humanity / not just rules but morality." In February, BRI and iCivics named Oliver a Civic Silver Star Award winner, earning a $2,000 grant for his school as part of the Civic Star Challenge. EdNC profiled his story this week. It's one of the best portraits of civics education working exactly as it should. Read it here:  https://lnkd.in/evGm4ciu  #EdNC  #CivicStarChallenge  #AwesomeTeachers

The Declaration of Independence declared that all men are created equal. Elizabeth Cady Stanton and Lucretia Mott read that and had a question: all men, or all people? Their answer was the Declaration of Sentiments, introduced at the Seneca Falls Convention in 1848. It borrowed the exact language of the Declaration of Independence, then amended it: all men AND women are created equal. It wasn't a rejection of the Founding ideals, but a demand that America live up to them. BRI's A250 Mini Documentary tells the story of the convention that launched a 70-year movement, rooted, from the very beginning, in the principles of the Declaration. Watch it here →  https://bit.ly/4onYo30  #SenecaFalls  #AmericanHistory  #A250

Everyone knows "no taxation without representation." The grievance was never really about the first word. Zachary Deibel, Assistant Professor at Virginia Military Institute, makes the point in Episode 17: the Continental Congress wasn't primarily upset about taxes. They were upset about representation, the fourth word of the motto that tends to get glossed over. No taxation had been enough to get people protesting. But no representation, in a government supposedly built on English liberty, was enough to launch a revolution. Discover more and join us for Constitution Day Live:  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M New episodes every Monday, Wednesday, and Friday.  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #ConsentOfTheGoverned

Ever since its drafting, the Declaration of Independence has sparked debate. First it was a dispute about separation from the Crown; later it was an argument about equality and its meaning. Our American debateusing the Declaration as a touchstone for our principleshas driven abolition, women's suffrage, and the civil rights movement. This debate continues today as we get ready to celebrate our nations 250th birthday. Divided Over the Declaration, the new book by BRIs David Bobb and Tony Williams, traces 250 years of Americans debating what all men are created equal  means, and who it includes. This story illustrates how each generation has turned to the Declaration as a standard worth fighting for. The debate over the Declarations promises is alive today. The Bill of Rights Institute exists to make sure the next generation knows how to have it, rigorously, honestly, and without flinching. Divided Over the Declaration releases today! Support BRI and claim your signed copy:  https://bit.ly/3PUcqN4
